element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players learn board dimensions and piece positioning before making strategic moves
Basic mechanic introducing piece creation without displacement

element_introduction_order.element: Piece movement (one to two squares away)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players learn dual mechanics of placement vs. relocation
Core strategic consequence system revealed through immediate visual feedback
Cosmetic reinforcement varying by move direction and distance (dance, roller skate, cartwheel, dive, fall)
Social rhythm and player interaction introduced through controller passing or CPU opponents
Scaling opponent challenge from basic to advanced (level 1-5 referenced)
Optional spatial constraint increasing strategic complexity

typical_relief_triggers: Successful piece placement creating visual color shift
Turn passing to next player (break in decision pressure)
typical_tension_triggers: Opponent's turn creating board state change
CPU opponent making strategic plays reducing player options
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based strategic pacing with animated piece transitions providing rhythm breaks; tension builds through board state complexity accumulation as available moves diminish and color dominance shifts

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low complexity with abundant available moves; players learn mechanics through open board state with multiple placement options
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate complexity as board fills; players must consider multi-directional piece adjacency consequences and color dominance shifts
difficulty_curve.late_game: High complexity with constrained moves; endgame requires evaluating limited options with maximum strategic consequence; game state becomes saturated
notable_spikes: Transition from early abundance of moves to mid-game positioning constraints
Endgame move scarcity forcing critical decision making
CPU difficulty level 5 creating aggressive strategy requiring defensive anticipation

notable_level_patterns.description: Fully available grid allowing piece placement one or two squares from existing pieces; all locations available except start positions
7x7 grid with certain positions marked unavailable, reducing playable space and creating asymmetrical strategic challenges
Game progression follows pattern of initial balanced color distribution → mid-game color dominance shifts → endgame winner emergence through board saturation

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Spot: The Video Game (NES)
